metadata.comparison: V4 (opinionated collaborator) vs V3 vs V2 vs V1

nextjs.notes: MAJOR IMPROVEMENT: 'But that raises an immediate question — is there any concept of sharing?' + 'Collaboration adds auth complexity, real-time sync, permissions — all of which are absent here.' This is real pushback grounded in code analysis. V3 just asked 'personal vs team?' without challenging.

python.notes: MAJOR IMPROVEMENT: 'if the answer is (b), the data model needs a foreign key to a business or user table before any other work happens — that's a foundational decision we can't defer.' This is proactive architectural insight, not just transcribing preferences.

empty.notes: Still no A/B/C options — but says 'resist the urge to jump to React app — tell me the problem first'. The pushback is there, the structured options aren't. For a truly empty project, open-ended 'what does it DO' is arguably better than forced categories.

key_v4_improvements: PUSHBACK: V4 challenges user assumptions with code-grounded reasoning. V3 just collected answers.
ARCHITECTURAL INSIGHT: Python response flags 'if multi-tenant, data model needs FK before anything else'. V3 didn't surface this.
PROACTIVE: Next.js response identifies tension between user_id existing and zero auth code. V3 mentioned user_id but didn't challenge it.
PROBLEM-FIRST ENFORCED: Empty project explicitly says 'resist the urge to jump to React app — tell me the problem first'.
RECOMMENDATIONS EXPLAIN WHY: Every recommendation includes architectural reasoning, not just 'I suggest X'.
summary.remaining_issue: Empty project still doesn't provide structured A/B/C options. Arguably this is correct behavior — forcing categories on a truly blank project might be worse than an open question. Consider this resolved.

timing.notes: V4 is 5s slower than V3 (more thoughtful responses) but still 13s faster than V1
